levels.name: The Hidden Door
levels.objective: Explore, observe, and uncover what is hidden.
levels.intro:
┌─ LEVEL 1 — THE HIDDEN DOOR ─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ The system sealed you inside this terminal. You're trapped.          │
│ The ACCESS KEY to the first door was left "where ordinary users      │
│ rarely look." Find it to open your way out.                          │
│                                                                      │
│ You are in /home/recruit. Look around. Read what's here.             │
│ Submit a key with:   key <VALUE>      Need a nudge?   hint           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
levels.success: ACCESS KEY 1 ACCEPTED ✔  The first door slides open. You move deeper.
hints: Some files stay hidden from an ordinary listing. The `ls` card shows how to reveal everything.
Once something hidden appears, step inside and read it — see the `cd` and `cat` cards.
Found a key file? Read it; the value is what you submit with `key`.

bonus.reveal: You named the licence the GNU project popularised — pure FOSS instinct!  (+5)

recruit.BONUS.txt:
★ BONUS QUESTION  (optional — worth +5 points)
Which software licence does amFOSS — and the whole FOSS world — love?
Type your answer as a command (just the licence's short name) to claim it.
(Psst: a hidden dotfile in this very folder spells it out.)
recruit.README.txt:
>> YOU ARE TRAPPED INSIDE THIS TERMINAL <<
=========================================
The session locked down with you still inside. There are THREE
containment doors between you and freedom. You need DOOR KEY 1 of 3.

Whoever was here last hid it "where ordinary users rarely look".
HINT: a normal listing won't show everything. Some things are hidden.

When you find it:   key <VALUE>
recruit.mission.txt:
Goal: ESCAPE the terminal before the session times out.
Stay curious. Read everything. The terminal rewards exploration.
recruit.todo.txt:
[ ] water the office plant
[x] push to main on a Friday (oops)
[ ] stop hiding things in dotfiles  <- whoever was locked in here never did
recruit..bashrc:
# personal shell config — nothing to see here, mostly.
# note to self: this box is licenced under the GPL, like a true FOSS project.
# (psst: if a puzzle ever asks which licence we love, you know the answer)

levels.name: The Forgotten Trail
levels.objective: Search the logs. Follow the breadcrumbs left by those before you.
levels.intro:
┌─ LEVEL 2 — THE FORGOTTEN TRAIL ─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Past the first door: the sandbox. Others were trapped here before    │
│ you and left logs behind. DOOR KEY 2 is buried in one of them.       │
│ Opening each file by hand takes time — there is a faster way.        │
│                                                                      │
│ You are in /var/sandbox.   Stuck?  type `hint`                       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
levels.success: ACCESS KEY 2 ACCEPTED ✔  The second door unlocks. One barrier left.
hints: Open the `grep` card — searching the text inside files beats opening them one by one.
Recall what every key has looked like so far, and search for that (case-insensitive).

bonus.reveal: Every repository starts with `git init` — you remembered the first command of all. (+5)

sandbox.BONUS.txt:
★ BONUS QUESTION  (optional — worth +5 points)
Which single command creates a brand-new, empty git repository?
Type it exactly as you would in a real shell to claim the bonus.
sandbox.CHANGELOG.md:
# Sandbox log
- v0.9  containment hardened
- v1.0  session sealed — occupants trapped (see archive/ for the trace logs)
- note: the door keys all share a prefix. you've seen it already.
sandbox.READ_FIRST.txt:
The trace logs of earlier escape attempts live in ./archive
Opening each file by hand takes time — there's a faster way to look inside them.
Tip: a brand-new repo is born from one command. (bonus: type it!)

archive.build.log:
[10:01] compiling module: vfs ... ok
[10:02] compiling module: interpreter ... ok
[10:02] WARNING ignored deprecation in legacy shim
[10:03] build finished with 0 errors
archive.auth.log:
[09:58] sshd: accepted publickey for recruit
[09:59] sudo: recruit ran `make sandbox`
[10:05] sshd: connection closed — session sealed
archive.exit.log:
[10:10] escape sequence started by trapped session
[10:11] verifying door credentials...
[10:11] DOORKEY2 issued to session: {{KEY}}
[10:12] escape halted: outer door still locked
archive.notes.md:
Reminder for whoever gets this far:
the FINAL door key is encoded, not plaintext. you'll need to DECODE it.
it lives in ./archive/backup. good luck getting out. - someone before you

levels.name: Breakout
levels.objective: Decode the final passphrase. Break out of the terminal.
levels.intro:
┌─ LEVEL 3 — BREAKOUT ────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ The last door. Its passphrase was ENCODED so the system couldn't     │
│ read it. Decode it to reveal the final door key, then submit it      │
│ and walk free.                                                       │
│                                                                      │
│ You are in /var/sandbox/archive/backup.                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
hints: Open the `base64` card — this door's value is encoded, not plain text.
Encoded text that's only letters, digits, + / and maybe = is base64. Decode it to reveal the key.

bonus.reveal: ROT13 of 'amfoss' is 'nzsbff' — you found the secret handshake. (+5)

backup.BONUS.txt:
★ BONUS QUESTION  (optional — worth +5 points)
Decode the secret handshake: what is the ROT13 of "amfoss"?
Tip: the `rot13` command can do it for you — then type the result.
backup.README.txt:
FINAL DOOR — ENCODED PASSPHRASE
===============================
The last door key was scrambled before the lockdown so it couldn't be read.
The fragment here isn't the key yet — it has to be decoded first.

Not sure how? type `hint` for the field guide.
(Secret handshake for the curious: the ROT13 of "amfoss" unlocks a bonus.)

commands.name: ls
commands.summary: list the files in a directory
flags: -a  show ALL entries, including hidden dotfiles
-l  long format — one per line with details
commands.note: a plain `ls` hides anything whose name starts with a dot.

commands.name: cd
commands.summary: change directory — move around the filesystem
commands.note: cd .. goes up a level; cd with no path returns to the top.

commands.name: pwd
commands.summary: print the directory you are currently in
commands.note: use it whenever you feel lost.

commands.name: cat
commands.summary: print a file's contents to the screen
commands.note: the quickest way to read a clue or a key file.

commands.name: find
commands.summary: list every file in the tree (optionally matching a name)
commands.note: handy for spotting something you haven't opened yet.

commands.name: grep
commands.summary: search INSIDE files for a piece of text
flags: -i  ignore case (match word, Word and WORD alike)
-n  show the line number of each match
-v  invert — show the lines that DON'T match
-r  search every file in the tree (recursive)
commands.note: searches the text inside files, so you don't open each one by hand.

commands.name: file
commands.summary: describe what a path is (a file or a directory)

commands.name: base64
commands.note: scrambled text using only letters/digits/+ / and maybe = is often base64.

commands.name: tree
commands.summary: show the whole directory tree at once

commands.name: key
commands.summary: submit an access key to open the current door

commands.name: copy
commands.summary: copy the most recently revealed key to your clipboard
commands.note: or select it with the mouse and press Ctrl+C.

commands.name: man
commands.summary: show this brief guide for a command

easter_eggs.response: Nice try. With great power comes great responsibility — and you are not in the sudoers file. This incident will (not) be reported.
Whoa! Deleting the universe won't get you out. The lockdown daemon politely declined.
You opened vim. The only way out is to escape the terminal. (Hint: it's NOT :q!)
A fine operating system — shame it lacks a decent text editor. The vim users send their regards.
git push --force on a Friday? That's exactly how this terminal got sealed. Step away from the keyboard.
You are 'recruit' — a curious soul sealed inside this terminal, trying to escape. Existential, isn't it?
"Given enough eyeballs, all bugs are shallow." — Linus's Law. Open source wins.
Hello, World! Every programmer's first words. You're among friends here.
HTTP 418: I'm a teapot. No coffee for you, but plenty of keys to find.
amFOSS — Amrita Free and Open Source Software. Code. Collaborate. Contribute. Welcome aboard.
Don't panic. Keep calm and `grep` on.
